mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 7 Series 740i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 740i engine typically costs £2,500–£7,000 supply only depending on generation, with older M60/M62 units lower and the B58 higher. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,800. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your 740i.

It depends which generation your 740i is. The E38 M62 is the most reliable V8 with timing chain guides as its main weakness. The G11/G70 B58 is the most refined and reliable overall. The N54 and N62 are the most maintenance‑sensitive. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 740i engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: E32 M60: Nikasil liner degradation. E38 M62: timing chain guide failure. E65 N62: valve stem seal failure. F01 N54: HPFP failure. F01 N55: water pump. G11/G70 B58: coolant system and PCV valve. Each generation has its own character and watchpoints.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a 740i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and generation. An E38 740i is a £3,000–£10,000 vehicle — a £3,000–£4,000 recon is 30–133% of the car's value. For lower‑value examples, replacement may exceed our 70% BMW scrap threshold. A G11/G70 740i is a £20,000–£50,000 vehicle — a £6,000–£7,000 recon is 12–35% of the car's value, making replacement viable. Evaluate carefully based on your car's generation and condition.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 740i engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The 740i covers M60 (E32), M62/M62TU (E38), N62 (E65), N54/N55 (F01), and B58 (G11/G70). Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm which one you have for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I prevent the common failures on a 740i?

acceptedAnswer.text: On M62: timing chain guides replacement at 100,000 miles is recommended. On N62: regular oil changes with BMW LL‑01 spec oil can extend valve stem seal life. On N54: use high‑quality fuel and have HPFP inspected. On B58: regular coolant system checks and BMW‑spec oil changes. At the first sign of rattle, blue smoke or rough running, book an inspection immediately.

problems.title: Nikasil Cylinder Liner Degradation (M60, E32 1992–1994)
problems.symptoms: Misfires, rough idle, loss of compression, increased oil consumption, engine warning light.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if misfires occur

problems.recommendation: The M60 V8's Nikasil cylinder liners can degrade when exposed to high‑sulphur fuel. If you experience misfires or rough running, have a compression test performed immediately. Many early M60 engines have been replaced; if yours hasn't, budget for a bottom‑end rebuild or replacement.

problems.title: Timing Chain Guide Failure (M62 / M62TU, E38 1995–2001)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start, metallic ticking, engine warning light, loss of power.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught before chain jumps)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The M62's timing chain guides are the defining weakness — they become brittle and can shatter, causing the chain to jump. If you hear a rattling noise on cold start, have the guides inspected immediately. Regular oil changes with the correct spec are essential. Many owners replace the guides preventatively around 100,000 miles.

problems.title: Valve Stem Seal Failure (N62, E65 2002–2008)
problems.symptoms: Blue smoke on startup, excessive oil consumption (1L per 1,000 miles+), fouled spark plugs.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught before catalytic converter damage)
problems.recommendation: The N62 V8 is notorious for valve stem seal failure — the seals harden and allow oil to leak into the combustion chamber. If you see blue smoke on startup or excessive oil consumption, have the valve stem seals inspected. Replacing them is labour‑intensive but far cheaper than a full engine replacement. Some owners use oil additives to temporarily reduce consumption, but this is not a permanent fix.

problems.title: High‑Pressure Fuel Pump Failure (N54, F01 2008–2012)
problems.symptoms: Long crank on start, rough idle, loss of power, engine stalling, engine warning light.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if symptoms appear

problems.recommendation: The N54's HPFP is its defining weakness — a well‑documented issue that plagued early cars. If you experience long cranking or loss of power, have the fuel pressure tested. BMW issued recalls for this, but many cars are now out of warranty. Replacing the HPFP with a revised unit is the permanent fix.

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 740i? Check These First
buyIf: * E32 M60: Nikasil liners replaced or compression test passed
* E38 M62: timing chain guides replaced or inspected
* E65 N62: valve stem seals replaced (or budget for it)
* F01 N54: HPFP replaced with revised unit
* Full service history with BMW-spec oil for the correct era
avoidIf: * E32: misfires or rough running — Nikasil may be degrading — walk away
* E38: cold‑start rattle — timing chain guides may be failing
* E65: blue smoke on startup — valve stem seals are failing
* F01 N54: long cranking — HPFP may be failing

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 740i badge is a journey through BMW flagship engineering — from the first V8s to the modern B58 straight‑six. The E38 M62 is the most reliable V8; the G11/G70 B58 is the most refined and reliable overall. The N54 and N62 are the most maintenance‑sensitive. Buy on generation‑specific history — and never ignore a cold‑start rattle or blue smoke.

cta.label: → Compare 740i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 7 Series 740i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling (M62 timing chain guides) or blue smoke (N62 valve stem seals)
* Misfires (E32) — Check for rough running; Nikasil liner degradation indicator
* Cranking Time (N54) — Check for long cranking; HPFP failure indicator
* Service History — including which generation (E32/E38/E65/F01/G11/G70) and confirmed maintenance
* Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles? (N62 valve stem seals)
* Coolant Level (B58) — Check for unexplained loss; water pump may be failing
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures
